topic: Introduction to the conceptual foundations of functions, focusing on reusability, abstraction, and modularity through a comparison between p5.js and the native Canvas API.

concepts.name: reusability
concepts.explanation: The idea that a piece of code can be used more than once in different locations or with different settings.
concepts.why_it_matters: It prevents you from having to write the same complex logic over and over again.

concepts.name: abstraction
concepts.explanation: Separating code from its immediate context or hiding complex implementation details behind a simpler interface.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows programmers to focus on what they want to achieve (like drawing a shape) rather than the tedious technical steps required by the computer.

concepts.name: modularity
concepts.explanation: The ability to bundle a whole bunch of complex actions into a single, manageable function call.
concepts.why_it_matters: It makes programs easier to organize and read by breaking them down into smaller, functional units.

concepts.name: function call
concepts.explanation: The act of actually running or using an existing function to perform a task.
concepts.why_it_matters: This is how we actually trigger the behaviors we want in our code.

code_examples.demonstrates: A simple p5.js function call to draw an ellipse with x, y, width, and height.
code_examples.common_issue: Forgetting a parameter or using the wrong number of arguments.

code_examples.demonstrates: The verbose, low-level native Canvas API required to draw a shape without the p5.js abstraction.
code_examples.common_issue: Forgetting to call beginPath() or stroke(), which results in nothing being drawn.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The code only draws a shape in one specific location and size, making it hard to change.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Lack of reusability (hard-coding values instead of using functions).
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Use functions to allow the same logic to be applied with different parameters.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: Shapes not appearing on the screen even though coordinates are correct.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: In native Canvas API, forgetting to call the 'stroke()' or 'fill()' command after defining a path.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Always call stroke() or fill() to actually render the path you've defined.

key_moments.description: The instructor warns that if a function is too limited (only one size/location), it loses its value for reusability.
Comparing the simple p5.js ellipse call to the complex native Canvas API to illustrate abstraction.
The instructor distinguishes between 'calling' a function (using it) and 'defining' a function (creating your own), setting the stage for the next lesson.

prerequisites: Familiarity with p5.js built-in functions (like setup and draw)
student_questions: What is the difference between calling a function and defining one?
Why do I have to use so many lines of code just to draw a circle in vanilla JS?
How do functions make my code more reusable?
Why is p5.js easier to use than the native Canvas API?
What does it mean when someone says my code isn't modular?
I can draw an ellipse, but how do I make it do something more complex without rewriting everything?
